data.title: First Solar shines but most solar names fall sharply as tariffs hit southeast Asia supply
data.description:
First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) +3.5% in Thursday's trading but most solar shares trade lower after President Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs on imports to the U.S., including heavy levies on countries that are major manufacturers of solar products.

Tariffs on southeast Asia countries, as well as further action against Chinese products, could have the most obvious impact on the U.S. solar industry, with Vietnam slapped with a 46% tariff, Thailand suffers a 36% hit, Malaysia gets 24% and Cambodia is hit with a 49% tariff, as well as a further 34% levy on Chinese goods.

First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) rises as the only U.S.-headquartered company among the world's largest solar manufacturers, but other leading solar and alternative energy stocks are posting big losses, including Enphase Energy (ENPH) -7.9%, Solaredge Technologies (SEDG) -8.8%, Sunrun (RUN) -10.7%, Array Technologies (ARRY) -10%, Canadian Solar (CSIQ) -5.2%, Shoals Technologies (SHLS) -7%, Sunnova Energy (NOVA) -5.5%, JinkoSolar (JKS) -4.9%, Daqo New Energy (DQ) -9.9%, Bloom Energy (BE) -8.2%, Plug Power (PLUG) -6.4%.

ETFs: (TAN), (ICLN), (QCLN), (PBW), (PBD), (ACES), (CNRG), (ERTH), (SMOG)

The current anti-dumping and countervailing duty tariffs which have raised the price of solar cells entering the country from the southeast Asian countries already have reduced the volume of imports compared with 2024, according to the U.S. Department of Commerce.

Southeast Asian countries and South Korea still account for the overwhelming majority of solar cells entering the U.S. to supply its growing module manufacturing industry, which may now become more expensive, PV-Tech.org reports.

While the U.S. has established ~50 GW of PV module manufacturing capacity, according to the Solar Energy Industries Association, the components needed to supply domestic factories are still mostly imported from Southeast Asia, with cell production lagging significantly behind module manufacturing capacity.

data.title: Small Cap ETFs get clobbered as Trump’s tariffs rattle markets
data.description:
Wall Street's major indices plunged into the red on Thursday, with investors reacting to President Trump's latest tariff announcement, which has sent shockwaves through the market. While the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has bore the brunt of the losses, small-cap exchange traded funds fared even worse, sinking to even lower levels.

Below, Seeking Alpha highlighted the 10 largest small-cap ETFs by market capitalization, detailing their rankings and specific price movements for the day.

data.title: U.S. recession odds on prediction market Kalshi spike to highest level since September
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's sweeping reciprocal tariffs have sent shockwaves through global financial markets on Thursday, while further souring U.S. growth estimates and sharply driving up the recession expectations.

The odds of a U.S. recession this year have surged to 54%, as per New York-based financial exchange and prediction market Kalshi. That's the highest level since late September last year. 

Kalshi also noted that U.S. recession odds in Q1 2025 had been as low as 17%.

According to Fitch Ratings, Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs result in an overall U.S. effective tariff rate last seen over a century ago in 1909. They also "significantly" raise U.S. recession risks. 

"The president’s highly anticipated tariff announcement...delivered at the very hawkish end of the range of expected outcomes ... By our calculations this takes the average effective tariff rate from what had been prior to (yesterday’s) announcement around 10% to just over 23%," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said.

Feroli also said that the reciprocal tariffs could boost personal consumption expenditure prices by 1% to 1.5% this year.

"The resulting hit to purchasing power could take real disposable personal income growth in 2Q-3Q into negative territory, and with it the risk that real consumer spending could also contract in those quarters. This impact alone could take the economy perilously close to slipping into recession," he added. 

U.S. stocks (SP500)(COMP:IND)(DJI) cratered on Thursday as sentiment globally was hammered by Trump's tariffs.

Here are some popular exchange-traded funds that track Wall Street's major averages: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:UDOW), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: US Energy Department seeking to use federal lands for data centers: report
data.description:
The U.S. Energy Department has announced plans to use land owned by the agency to construct artificial intelligence data centers, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

According to the report, the department said it’s considering using thousands of acres of federal land around the country that could be used to quickly develop the facilities, partly because the government will be able to fast-track the nuclear power reactors and other power plants that would run the data centers. 

“The global race for AI dominance is the next Manhattan project,” Energy Secretary Chris Wright was quoted as saying in a statement. “The Department of Energy is taking important steps to leverage our domestic resources to power the AI revolution, while continuing to deliver affordable, reliable and secure energy to the American people.”

The report said the department has determined 16 sites that are ideal to construct data centers, including the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory in Central New Jersey, nuclear sites in South Carolina and Texas, and former uranium enrichment facilities in Kentucky and Ohio. 

Wright plans to discuss the plans on Thursday with Colorado Gov. Jared Polis during a visit to the National Renewable Energy Laboratory in Golden, Bloomberg reported. 

The report said officials are looking for input from developers of data centers and others to create public-private partnerships in AI development. The department is aiming to have data centers operating on its land by the close of 2027, the report added. 

Neither the Energy Department nor Polis’ office immediately responded to requests for comment by Seeking Alpha.

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

Utilities ETFs: (NYSEARCA:XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI).

data.title: Reaction Roundup: SA analysts weigh in on new Trump tariffs
data.description:
Below is a roundup of views expressed by Seeking Alpha analysts regarding the latest round of tariffs announced by the Trump administration.

Pearl Gray Equity and Research: The growing protectionist stance of President Trump will likely diminish short-term consumer sentiment, forming an unwelcoming scenario, especially consider that the U.S. economy is arguably in late cycle growth. However, we foresee solid risk-off opportunities, especially in intermediate-to-long term treasuries, selective investment grade corporate debt, CLOs and high-quality REITs.

Oakoff Investments: Trump's unexpected 10% baseline tariff on all imports shocked markets, leading to increased volatility and potential inflationary pressures, with inflation possibly rising to 5%....Given the heightened risks and market volatility, I'm downgrading the S&P 500 to "Hold", but recommend considering buying the dip if the index drops by 10% from current levels.

Ricardo Fernandez: The main beneficiaries of these tariffs may have already factored in higher margins, whereas, in my view, the digital economy is not immune and may face cross-border tax increases. The utility sector may be the best place to ride out the storm if not in a money market.

Geneva Investor: Trump aims to reclaim manufacturing jobs (11% of GDP) via tariffs, but this overlooks the dominant service sector (75%+ of GDP), where the US has a trade surplus (e.g., $109B with EU). This looks to me like the worst deal ever conceived by an administration. I rate Trump's economic policy so far a D-, not an F, based on the hope that it is only phase 1 of a broader strategy where the real objective is inducing a quick US recession and force a Fed pivot.

MTS Insights: White House officials have shared that patience for Commerce Secretary Lutnick (one of Trump’s main tariff champions) is already running thin as unpopular tariff policies roil the markets. Resistance to these economically painful policies is likely to bring about some moderation that will eventually calm markets. Watch out for these signals that could market a turn in sentiment for the better.

James Foord: Tariffs will no doubt have a detrimental effect on the economy, failing to re-ignite American manufacturing and possibly contributing to deflation and recession. But, this provides the perfect backdrop for the Fed to step in and ease, which may help prevent a much worse crisis, a refinancing debt crisis….Things look ugly now, understandably, but in the not-so-distant future, we could see a double tailwind for markets of reduced tariffs and more liquidity from the Fed. This is ultimately a time to buy the dip, with great bargains available for long-term investors.

Daniel Jones: These are certainly scary times in my opinion. Market conditions are incredibly uncertain, but my belief is that we should expect a meaningful decline in the economic welfare of the US. It is obviously possible that one side or the other will bend under pressure. But if that does not transpire, stagflation and a decline in the size of the economy would not be unthinkable.

ING Economic and Financial Analysis: We still think the radical change in global trade relationships in the first few months of Trump’s presidency argues for a strengthening of the dollar across the board, but if US data keeps deteriorating and the US is forced to water down its tariff threat, the dollar would weaken without any artificial intervention. In this environment - and given the risks of more equity losses and lower long-end US yields - the yen may well emerge as the most attractive currency in G10 in the near term.

data.title: Hanover Insurance appoints COO
data.description:
• The Hanover Insurance Group (NYSE:THG) announced on Thursday the appointment of Richard Lavey as COO.
  • In the newly created role, Lavey will work for further development of business strategy, and drive even greater integration and collaboration between business and technology organizations.
  • The new role comes in addition to his current responsibilities of leading personal lines and core commercial.

data.title: Mortgage rates settle, purchase applications soar
data.description:
Home purchase applications soared this week as mortgage rates settled in, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.64% as of April 3, down from 6.65% last week and 6.82% in the year-ago period.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.82%, compared to 5.89% a week ago and 6.06% in the prior year.

"Over the last month, the 30-year fixed-rate has settled in, making only slight moves in either direction. This stability is reassuring, and borrowers have responded with purchase application demand rising to the highest growth rate since late last year," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

data.title: S&P 500 craters and these 10 names are driving the index lower
data.description:
The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) faced a significant selloff on Thursday, driven by concerns over President Trump's tariff policies, which have ignited widespread fear on Wall Street and pushed markets lower.

By midday Thursday, the S&P 500 had dropped 3.7%, hovering around 5,461, and now stands down 6.9% for the year.

data.title: Lululemon shares roiled by Vietnam exposure
data.description:
• Shares of Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU) took a dive on Thursday, falling to an eight-month low as the company’s presence in Vietnam exposes its bottom-line to President’s Trump’s 46% tariff on merchandise imported from the Southeast Asian country.
  • Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU), along with other companies like Nike (NKE), Under Armour (UA) (UAA), and Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF), moved much of their manufacturing into Vietnam from China to sidestep the worsening trade relationship between Washington and Beijing.
  • The company began moving production to Vietnam in 2016, reducing its manufacturing in China by nearly half in the subsequent three years.
  • While a third of Lululemon’s (NASDAQ:LULU) production is conducted in Vietnam, the company also does business in Sri Lanka (11%), Cambodia (9%), and Indonesia (6%).
  • Shares are down 11% at midday.

data.title: Msc industrial projects improved Q3 operating margins amid core customer growth initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MSC Industrial Direct Co., Inc. (MSM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Erik Gershwind highlighted a 4.7% year-over-year decline in average daily sales but noted improvement through the quarter, particularly in January and February. Execution improvements and favorable supplier rebates were key contributors to gross and adjusted operating margins reaching the high end of expectations.
  • The company reported a 24% year-over-year increase in in-plant programs and a 9% growth in installed vending machines, totaling over 28,000 units. Enhanced website upgrades improved product discovery, checkout efficiency, and personalization, showing early signs of increased customer engagement.
  • President and COO Martina McIsaac detailed proactive steps to navigate tariffs, leveraging a diverse product portfolio, including 200,000 Made in USA SKUs. Marketing efforts featuring these products increased website traffic significantly, with a 60-fold rise in views on cost-saving case studies.
  • CFO Kristen Actis-Grande reported $892 million in sales, a 4.7% decline year-over-year, with public sector sales growing 13.2%. Adjusted operating expenses were $302 million, reflecting productivity gains offset by personnel costs and investments.

Outlook

  • Management expects Q3 2025 average daily sales to decline 2% to flat year-over-year. Adjusted operating margin is projected between 8.7% and 9.3%, with gross margin guidance at 40.9%, plus or minus 20 basis points.
  • Full-year assumptions include $90 million to $95 million in depreciation, $100 million to $110 million in capital expenditures, and a tax rate between 24.5% and 25%. Free cash flow conversion is targeted at 100% of net income.

Financial Results

  • Q2 gross margin was reported at 41%, a 50-basis-point year-over-year decline due to higher-priced inventories and customer mix. A sequential 30-basis-point improvement was attributed to supplier rebates.
  • GAAP EPS for Q2 was $0.70, with adjusted EPS at $0.72, compared to $1.10 and $1.18, respectively, in the prior year.
  • Free cash flow conversion was 63% for Q2 and 125% fiscal year-to-date.

Q&A

  • Wolfe Research’s Ryan Cooke inquired about the sales outlook and margin progression. CFO Actis-Grande noted the guidance assumes stable macro conditions with improvements driven by share gain initiatives. Supplier rebates from Q2 are not expected to recur in Q3.
  • Stephens’ Tommy Moll asked about March price increases and potential for further adjustments. Actis-Grande clarified a 0.5% pricing benefit was implemented for products imported from China, representing a small portion of total COGS.
  • KeyBanc Capital Markets’ Ken Newman sought insights on end-market demand. CEO Gershwind observed continued softness in automotive and heavy truck sectors but highlighted aerospace as a resilient segment with positive outlook.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, raising concerns about end-market visibility and macroeconomic pressures. Questions focused on margin resilience and growth in core customer segments.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing progress in execution and strategic initiatives. CEO Gershwind stated, “We are encouraged by improving execution and early signs of success in our growth initiatives.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited slightly improved sentiment, reflecting sequential improvements in key metrics and operational advancements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 adjusted EPS of $0.72 declined from $0.86 in Q1, while sales fell from $928 million, reflecting continued demand softness.
  • Q2 operating margin guidance highlighted a focus on controlling costs amid sales declines, compared to Q1’s stronger gross margin performance.
  • Management showed increased confidence in core customer growth and marketing initiatives, contrasting Q1’s focus on foundational improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff-related uncertainties and potential inflationary pressures were emphasized. Management outlined mitigation strategies, including proactive inventory management and pricing adjustments.
  • Analysts expressed concerns over soft demand in key manufacturing sectors and limited visibility on macroeconomic recovery.

Final Takeaway

MSC Industrial Direct continues to face a challenging demand environment but is taking measured steps to enhance execution and drive growth in core customer segments. While Q3 guidance reflects cautious optimism, management’s focus on strategic initiatives, including website upgrades and productivity improvements, positions the company for long-term recovery once macro conditions stabilize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
